pages:
Primary Breast Cancer  
Fiona Armitage  
Breast Clinical Nurse Specialist

Incident  
•Each month 5,000 women are diagnosed with 
breast cancer  
•The most common cancers in the world  
•Each year in the UK alone there are more than 
55,000 new cases  
•disease claims the lives of 11,500 women .(2015 -17)  
•Screening identifies early cancers  
•mammogram , which can spot cancers when 
they’re too small to see and feel.

Survival  
•Breast cancer outcomes are improving steadily 
with 5 year survival rates now over 80%  
• very low rates of loco -regional recurrence, 
quoted at 0.2 to 0.5% per year in the latest 
studies   
•These advances are multifactorial and due to 
improvements in surgery and adjuvant 
therapies, combined with earlier diagnosis 
due to screening

•Breast cancer often has no symptoms, but you 
may notice something you want the doctor to 
check. Keep an eye out for:  
•A painless lump in the breast  
•Changes in breast size or shape  
•Swelling in the armpit  
•Nipple changes or discharge  
•Breast pain can also be a symptom of cancer, but 
this isn’t common.

What is breast cancer  
•Breast cells divide and grow in an abnormal way  
•Breast cancer not one disease – several  
•Each breast cancer peculiar to that person  
•Primary breast cancer not travelled beyond the 
breast / axilla  
•Different features / Biology  
•Grade 1, 2, 3  
• Size  
•Type ductal, lobular, medullary , mucinous

How common it is?  
Breast cancer is the most common cancer in the UK. Around  55,200 people are 
diagnosed with breast cancer every year in the UK. That  is around 150 people a day.   
15 out  of 100 (15%) of all newly diagnosed cancers in the UK are breast cancer.

How common it is?  
 
•Breast cancer is the most common cancer in 
the UK.  
•Around  55,200 people are diagnosed with 
breast cancer every year in the UK.  
•That  is around 150 people a day.   
•15 out  of 100 (15%) of all newly diagnosed 
cancers in the UK are breast cancer.

What is breast cancer?  
 
•Breast cancer is the most common cancer in the UK.  
•It mainly affects women, but in rare cases men can get 
breast cancer too.  
•Breast cancer can cause symptoms such as a lump. 
However, a lump is not the only symptom of breast 
cancer  
•The earlier breast cancer is diagnosed, the easier it is 
to treat and often has better outcomes  
• This means it’s important for women to check their 
breasts  regularly and report any unusual changes to 
their GP .

Risk reducing factors  
•Women who breast -feed (6 months exclusively and up 
to 2 years or beyond partially ) 25%.  
•Low BMI and by getting exercise.  
•Cut back on the amount of alcohol consumed  
•Birth control pills and hormone therapy after 
menopause can increase the risk. But the risk seems to 
go back to normal after you stop these medications.  
•Good lifestyle choices can help survivors, too.  
•Research says physical activity can lower the chances 
your cancer will return. And it's a proven mood -
booster, too.

How common is breast cancer?  
 
•One in eight women in the UK will develop 
breast cancer in their lifetime.   
•Around 55,000 women and 370 men are 
diagnosed with breast cancer every year in the 
UK.  
•Around a further 7,000 people are diagnosed 
with ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) each year.

At what age does breast cancer 
occur?  
 •Breast cancer occurs mainly in older women.  
•Most women who are diagnosed with breast 
cancer are over the age of 50. However, it can 
occur in younger women too.  
•Most men who get breast cancer are over 60.   
•Breast cancer in teenagers  is extremely rare.

statistics  
•1:8 women in the UK  
•Most commen  cancer in theUK  
•Around 55,000 women and 370 men are 
diagnosed with breast cancer every year in the 
UK.  
•Around a further 7,000 people are diagnosed 
with ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS)  each year.

How does breast cancer start?  
•Breast cancer starts when cells in the breast 
begin to divide and grow in an abnormal way.  
•Breasts contains lobules,  the milk producing 
glands  
•Tubes called ducts carry milk to the nipple.   
•The most common type of breast cancer starts 
in the ducts.  
•Less commonly, breast cancer can start in the 
lobules

What grade is the breast cancer?  
 
•Cancer cells are given a grade according to how 
different they are to normal breast cells and how 
quickly they are growing.  
•There are three grades of invasive breast cancer:  
•grade 1  – looks most like normal breast cells and is 
usually slow -growing  
•grade 2  – looks less like normal cells and is growing 
faster  
•grade 3  – looks different to normal breast cells and is 
usually fast -growing  
•Grade is different to the stage of breast cancer.

Why does breast cancer occur?  
  
•Exactly why some people get breast cancer and others don't is 
not fully understood.  
•Research suggests that breast cancer is caused by a 
combination of lots of different factors.  
•The main things that increase someone’s risk of breast cancer 
– like being a woman or being older – are beyond our control.  
•Most breast cancers happen by chance. Having a relative with 
breast cancer doesn’t automatically mean your own risk is 
higher.  
•A small number of breast cancers happen because someone 
has inherited an altered version of a gene from one of their 
parents.

What are breast cancer stages?  
  
•The stage of a cancer describes  
–the size of the cancer and how far it has spread.  
–Your breast cancer may be described as stage 1, stage 2, stage 3 
or stage 4.  
–The stage takes into account:  
•the size of the cancer  
•whether the lymph nodes  are affected  
•if the cancer has spread to other parts of the body  
•MDT will look at the size and grade of the cancer, as well as 
the type of breast cancer  
•The biology of the breast cancer  will dictate decide the 
most appropriate treatment

Breast assessmnt  
•The breast mdt will look at the presentation  
–size  
– grade  of the cancer   
–type of breast cancer  
– to help decide the most appropriate treatment

Treatment options  
Generally, people with grade 3 invasive  breast cancers are more likely to be offered 
chemotherapy, to help destroy any cancer cells that may have spread as a result of the 
cancer being faster growing.  
Find out more about breast cancer and prognosis . 
Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS)  
There are also three grades of ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) , which are usually 
referred to as low, intermediate and high.

diagnosis  
•Triple assessment  
–Mammogramm  
–Ultrasound - breast and axilla  
–Biopsy

MDT  
•Multi disciplinary team  
–Core members surgeon, Radiologist, 
Histopathologist , Oncologist and CNS   
– Other HCP’s  
•Every week, every patient  
– Surgeon present the case  
–Radiologist Imaging - mammogram, U/S  
–Histopathologist  – histology  
–Oncologist – adjuvant therapy  
–CNS – key worker throughout primary treatment

2. What size is the breast cancer?  
The size of the breast cancer is measured at its widest point, usually in 
millimetres (mm). One inch equals about 25mm.  
If DCIS  and invasive breast cancer  are found together, the results will tell you 
their combined size (sometimes called ‘whole tumour size’) and how much there 
is of each. If both types are found together, only the details of the invasive breast 
cancer will be used by your specialist team to look at treatment and  prognosis 
(outlook).  
While in general smaller cancers may have a better outcome, size doesn’t always 
give the whole picture and is just one part of the overall results. A small cancer 
can be fast growing while a larger cancer may be slow growing, or it could be the 
other way around.

Treatment  
•The size and position of the cancer in relation to the 
breast size may affect surgical options  
•smaller cancers < 5cm it’s possible to have breast -
conserving surgery (also called wide local excision or 
lumpectomy).  
• larger cancer’s >5cm recommendation mastectomy +/ - 
reconstruction or chemotherapy before surgery (called 
neo-adjuvant or primary chemotherapy ) with the aim 
of shrinking the cancer before surgery.  
•The MDT will make a decision about whether to 
recommend chemotherapy depending on the size of 
the breast cancer and other features, such as whether 
or not the lymph nodes are affected

Other things to consider  
•Age 
•Receptors  
–ER sensitivity 3/5 bc population positive  
–HER2 sensitivity 1/5 population positive  
•Type, ductal, lobular, medullary, mucinous, 
tubular, papillary, metaplastic , padgets  and DCIS

Treatment options  
The size and position of the cancer in relation to your breast size may affect what 
operation you’re offered. For example, with smaller cancers it’s often possible to 
have breast -conserving surgery (also called wide local excision or lumpectomy).  
If you have a larger cancer (in relation to your breast size), your specialist team may 
recommend a mastectomy , or chemotherapy  before surgery (called neo -adjuvant 
or primary chemotherapy). This is sometimes given with the aim of shrinking the 
cancer before surgery.  
Your specialist team will make a decision about whether to recommend 
chemotherapy depending on the size of the breast cancer and other features, such 
as whether or not the lymph nodes are affected. Generally, people with larger 
breast cancers (greater than 2cm) are more likely to be offered chemotherapy. This 
is because larger cancers may have been there longer before being found and so 
may have had more chance to spread.

Types of breast cancer  
•Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS)  
•Lobular carcinoma in situ  
•Invasive ductal  (ductal breast cancer) 75%  
•Invasive lobular carcinoma 15-20%  
•Medullary carcinoma 5% 
•Mucinous (colloid) carcinoma 5% 
•Tubular carcinoma 1-2% 
•Papillary carcinoma 1-2% 
•Metaplastic breast cancer (MBC) 1% >60 typically negative for ER, 
PR, and HER2.  
•Phyllodes  tumors   
•Mammary Paget disease (MPD) 1-4% 
•Inflammatory breast cancer

Treatment options  
The size and position of the cancer in relation 
to your breast size may affect what operation 
you’re offered. For example, with smaller 
cancers it’s often possible to have breast -
conserving surgery (also called wide local 
excision or lumpectomy).  
If you have a larger cancer (in relation to your 
breast size), your specialist team may 
recommend a mastectomy , or chemotherapy  
before surgery (called neo -adjuvant or primary 
chemotherapy). This is sometimes given with 
the aim of shrinking the cancer before surgery.  
Your specialist team will make a decision about 
whether to recommend chemotherapy 
depending on the size of the breast cancer and 
other features, such as whether or not the 
lymph nodes are affected. Generally, people 
with larger breast cancers (greater than 2cm) 
are more likely to be offered chemotherapy. 
This is because larger cancers may have been 
there longer before being found and so may 
have had more chance to spread.

Treating breast cancer and reducing the 
risk of recurrence  
 
•Surgery  
•Radiotherapy  
•Chemotherapy  
•Hormone treatment  
•Targeted therapy  
•Bisphosphonates

TREATMENT  
•Surgery is considered primary treatment for 
early -stage breast cancer; many patients are 
cured with surgery alone.  
•AIM -complete resection of the primary 
tumour with negative margins to reduce the 
risk of local recurrences and pathologic 
staging of the tumour and axillary lymph 
nodes (ALNs) to provide necessary prognostic 
information.

Adjuvant therapy  
•Designed to treat micrometastatic  disease ( ie, 
breast cancer cells that have escaped the 
breast and regional lymph nodes but which 
have not yet had an established identifiable 
metastasis).  
•Adjuvant treatment for breast cancer involves 
radiotherapy and systemic therapy (including 
a variety of chemotherapy, hormonal and 
monoclonal antibodies).

Adjuvant therapy  
•Treatment is aimed at reducing the risk of 
future recurrence, thereby reducing breast 
cancer -related morbidity and mortality. 
Depending on the model of risk reduction, 
adjuvant therapy has been estimated to be 
responsible for 35 -72% of the reduction in 
mortality

The patient  
• the patient is @ the Centre of care  
•Individual  
•Complex  
•Don’t assume  
•listen  
•Hope you all take something from today to 
enrich the patient journey

CNS 
•Role Support early primary breast cancer 
individuals with diagnosis of breast cancer and 
throughout the care pathway.

•Breast cancer outcomes are improving steadily 
with 5 year survival rates now over 80% and 
very low rates of loco -regional recurrence, 
quoted at 0.2 to 0.5% per year in the latest 
studies.  These advances are multifactorial 
and due to improvements in surgery and 
adjuvant therapies, combined with earlier 
diagnosis due to screening.
